records.ety: [OE. voide, OF. voit, voide, vuit, vuide, F. vide, fr. (assumed) LL. vocitus, fr. L. vocare, an old form of vacare to be empty, or a kindred word. Cf. Vacant, Avoid.]

defs: Containing nothing; empty; vacant; not occupied; not filled.
quotes: The earth was without form, and void.
I 'll get me to a place more void.
I 'll chain him in my study, that, at void hours, I may run over the story of his country.

defs: Having no incumbent; unoccupied; -- said of offices and the like.
quotes: Divers great offices that had been long void.

defs: Being without; destitute; free; wanting; devoid; as, void of learning, or of common use.
quotes: A conscience void of offense toward God.
He that is void of wisdom despiseth his neighbor.

defs: Not producing any effect; ineffectual; vain.
quotes: [My word] shall not return to me void, but it shall accomplish that which I please.
I will make void the counsel of Judah.

defs: Containing no immaterial quality; destitute of mind or soul.
Of no legal force or effect, incapable of confirmation or ratification; null. Cf. Voidable, 2.

quotes: Pride, where wit fails, steps in to our defense, And fills up all the mighty void of sense.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Voided; p. pr. & vb. n. Voiding.]

defs: To remove the contents of; to make or leave vacant or empty; to quit; to leave; as, to void a table.
quotes: If they will fight with us, bid them come down, Or void the field.

defs: To throw or send out; to evacuate; to emit; to discharge; as, to void excrements.
quotes: A watchful application of mind in voiding prejudices.
With shovel, like a fury, voided out The earth and scattered bones.

defs: To render void; to make to be of no validity or effect; to vacate; to annul; to nullify.
quotes: After they had voided the obligation of the oath he had taken.
It was become a practice . . . to void the security that was at any time given for money so borrowed.
